title: What Will Markets Be Forced to Price In Next?

morning. Welcome back here to Opening Bell. I'm Nicole Petallides getting you ready for a new trading day. A new second half of the year. Stocks are under a bit of pressure here as you look at U.S. stock index futures. But an incredible first half the S&P was up about 15%. The Nasdaq up over 27% in the first half of this year. Joining me now, Darius Dell, founder and CEO at 42 macro. To take a look at what we can expect going forward. Good morning to you Terry Steel. Some of your thoughts. I mean, people were maybe surprised by some of the great numbers. We had tariffs. We had geopolitical events. We had oil you know, surging well over 100 bucks and all of it sort of played out. And here we are with some wins. What do you expect here to continue. What's your outlook going forward near term and long term. Well thanks for having me Nicole. It's always a pleasure to be here. So the first thing I'd say is the market has to cluster around and price in what we think are three nodes in the distribution of probable market policy and ultimately economic outcomes. Number one, we think what's happening right now and what's been happening is peak inflation. Then we're going to have to price in peak fed policy uncertainty. We think that's ultimately bearish initially, but ultimately will be very bullish for the markets in about 2 or 3 quarters time. And then ultimately we're going to see peak CapEx from a rate of change perspective. The market is very clearly telling the mag seven stocks to stop accelerating CapEx into declining token usage and accelerating Dram prices. There needs to be a modulation of their demand and which will ultimately ripple through the broader financial markets. From the perspective of earnings revisions. So if it's not the Mag seven, you know, sort of slow down on that CapEx spending, we want to see some ROI return on investment monetization, but folks are still believers in these hyperscalers, are they not? What do you think? In our view, we think they shouldn't be. These respects to the longer term view on markets, because ultimately, if everyone's views on AI is are actually proven correct, which we think they will be, then you're talking about a convergence of profitability, of margins of productivity, which will ultimately force a convergence in valuations between these hyperscaler stocks, which have had historically wide gross margins prior to now, and historic rates of free cash flow prior to now, you're going to force convergence in their operating metrics with the rest of the companies in the index and the rest of the companies abroad, because AI is going to diffuse across borders. So is is this a tech story? You know, the mag seven names or all the names because it sounds like you're cautious, right? You also talked about the potential for headwinds when it comes to monetary policy. We're going to get some breaking news on Kevin Warsh quotes as well, because some of the other names in tech did well. What do you suggest people do if they if they shouldn't be in those mag seven names? And I know you're going to bring us some sectors, but what should they be fully invested and if so, what? Well, fully invest in our opinion is a multi asset discussion with respect to the rest of the broader markets. You know we've been in this risk on reflation market regime where you want to keep your duration risk low, your credit risk high, your equity exposure high, and generally speaking, your exposure to things like metals and gold relatively relatively contained. And so that's exactly what's been happening. But our concern here is that the markets are going to transition from pricing in the rightmost tail of that, that mode, that multi modal distribution of probable outcomes, which is peak inflation to starting to price in other things, which and those other things are not as bullish, in fact explicitly bearish. If you think about the, the prospect of the fed tightening monetary policy to take some steam off the boil from the US core inflation cycle, and ultimately, we ultimately think this this this peak rate of change of AI CapEx could ripple through markets in a negative way as well. So we've been coaching investors to anticipate something like the summer of 1998 over the next 1 or 2 quarters. But ultimately, we think you got to buy that dip because the 42 macro paradigm see bull market is not over. And we are getting some breaking news. And maybe you can give me your interpretation or how you feel about Kevin Warsh and some of the comments. And J. Woods, who was on. I was speaking with him earlier, but he's been talking about that. He didn't really expect too much from Kevin Warsh. And it looks like Kevin Warsh is playing it close to the vest here. And some of his comments. Darius, we can break through these together. We'll chart a new course. We can make better decisions. Not going to give forward guidance. Well we sort of knew that. I hope that results of task forces will be a public good. It's up to the central bank to decide if AI is inflationary. AI boom is showing itself first and prominently in the U.S. this comes on the heels of yesterday when Beth Hammack was talking about the consumer data and saying, look, you know, if it holds up the way it does, maybe the policy is not restrictive enough. Some of your thoughts. Well, we know policy is not restrictive. If we look at our model for the market implied neutral rate, the fed is about two rate hikes behind the curve in accommodative territory right now. So the fed is actually compounding the problem with respect to the core inflation pressure in the US economy as it relates to the Fed's reaction function to AI, in our view, we ultimately think that the fed understands that AI is productivity enhancing over the long term will ultimately cost trend inflation to go down and create scope for Non-inflationary growth. But the path to getting there is the issue. Right now we're talking. We're currently at $800 billion of estimated AI CapEx here for 2026. That's about 2.5% of GDP. We're going to accelerate to at least according to the current sell side estimates, to about $1.2 trillion of AI CapEx next year. That's about 3.6% of GDP. So you have this massive aggregate demand shock coinciding with Pro-cyclical fiscal stimulus, coinciding with an accommodative fed and the monetary dynamics in the economy that are also moving in a direction that suggests this is a sticky inflation problem. So ultimately, the market's going to have to transition from pricing in right tail risk e is the most likely outcome. I'm sorry, rate hike. That's just going to compound the problem for the households the businesses and the interest rate sensitive sectors on the bottom of the K. In our view, we think the fed has ample scope to use its balance sheet policy to explicitly target folks like us at the top of the K, to take some steam off the boil. From the perspective of the the core inflationary pressure building up in this US economy. And so ultimately the fed who's who's willing to to explicitly target the financial markets and all the speculation and excesses in financial markets that are supporting income of folks like us. If the fed can do that, that will create the scope for them to ease much more materially starting in Q4, maybe even Q1 of next year, because that's ultimately what we think this is headed as it relates to the outcome of the five task force. Can we talk about some of your recommendations today? You looked globally, the global stock market. You looked at VTi and the Equal Weight Index, RSP. Tell me about those and why those should belong in a portfolio. Yeah. Well, since last fall we've been short max seven long. Basically everything else global equities the non max seven companies here in the US market as a function of our source of funds theme. Again we're going to see a convergence in productivity profitability margins and ultimately valuation. We'll talk about we're already seeing the convergence in productivity profitability margins. And so ultimately the next step of that trade is we're going to continue to see convergence of valuations between basically everything else relative to the Max seven, particularly as the Max seven is being punished by the market for for, you know, exacerbating its free cash flow decline. And so ultimately that we think that trade has legs. We've seen this movie source of funds movie before in financial markets. We saw China converge materially with U.S. equities after it was ascended into the WTO, as we had a unified global market for China. And then we also saw the source of funds moving in the European market back in the 90s, after the Maastricht treaty was signed, we saw massive compression in European credit spreads, you know, across sovereign sovereigns in Europe, you know, as a function of that, that adoption of that unified monetary policy. So ultimately, AI represents a unified, you know, basically system of generating profits that the tech companies are transferring from themselves to all the other companies in the index. And so ultimately, we think this trade has legs. I also thought it was interesting in your notes that you talked about liquidity, the repo market, the Fed's balance sheet, the strength of the Fed's balance sheet and geopolitical story when it comes to supply and demand. Can you tell me a little bit about that? Yeah. Well, so we've been in our core research thesis since the summer of 2023 is that there is a geopolitically driven supply demand imbalance in the Treasury securities market. And as a function of that, we've seen a variety of policy choices by both the Treasury and the fed to deal with this. Obviously, the fed has shortened the duration of its issuance and maintain dovish policy since then, rubber stamped by both Janet Yellen and and our former Treasury Secretary, Scott Bessent. And then we've also seen the fed maintain asymmetrically dovish monetary policy in the face of sticky well above target inflation throughout. Obviously the fed has cut interest rates 175 basis points since then. And as you know, and as obviously continuing to expand its balance sheet, starting with the reserve management purchases from last from last fall. So ultimately, we think that is the core driver of the of the main policy choices that investors, that the policy makers are making here in the U.S. economy. So ultimately, investors need to have a deep understanding of all the different vectors and nodes of that geopolitical disequilibrium, because that's where markets that's what's going to lead markets over the next few years, in our opinion. Okay. Darius Dale, great conversation. Really appreciate it. And a good setup and outlook for the second half of the year
